title: Routing Algorithms — Distance Vector vs Link State
examTip: Two main routing algorithm types: (1) Distance Vector (RIP, IGRP) — Bellman-Ford algorithm, each router shares its ENTIRE routing table with DIRECTLY CONNECTED neighbours periodically. Simple, slow convergence, count-to-infinity problem. (2) Link State (OSPF, IS-IS) — Dijkstra's SPF algorithm, each router floods its LOCAL link info to ALL routers, builds full topology map. Fast convergence, more memory/CPU, scalable with areas. Key formula: Bellman-Ford: d(u,v) = min over all w: {c(u,w) + d(w,v)}

blocks.label: Routing Algorithms — Distance Vector vs Link State
Algorithm: Bellman-Ford
blocks.note: d(u,v) = min over all w { cost(u,w) + d(w,v) }

blocks.label: Algorithm: Dijkstra's Shortest Path First (SPF)
blocks.note: Greedy expansion — always extends to lowest cost unvisited node

blocks.label: How DVR Works
sublabels: 1. Each router maintains a Distance Vector table: destination, next-hop, cost.
2. Periodically (every 30s in RIP), each router sends its ENTIRE routing table to all directly connected neighbours only.
3. Neighbours update their own tables using Bellman-Ford: if cost through me < your current best → update.

5. Count-to-Infinity Problem: If a link goes down, routers may loop indefinitely incrementing the cost. Fixed by: Split Horizon (don't advertise a route back where you learned it), Route Poisoning (set cost to 16/infinity when link fails), Hold-down timers.

blocks.label: How LSR Works
sublabels: 1. Each router discovers its DIRECTLY CONNECTED neighbours and their link costs using Hello packets.
2. Each router creates a Link State Advertisement (LSA) containing: router ID, neighbour list, link costs.
3. LSAs are FLOODED to ALL routers in the network (reliable flooding with sequence numbers).
4. Each router builds an identical Link State Database (LSDB) = full network topology map.
5. Each router independently runs Dijkstra's SPF algorithm on its LSDB to compute the shortest path tree and populate its routing table.

blocks.label: Comparison Table: Distance Vector vs Link State
Full routing table — to direct neighbours only
Local link state (LSA) — flooded to ALL routers
Fast — all routers get LSDB simultaneously (seconds)
High — stores LSDB, runs Dijkstra on every change
Large networks with hierarchical areas (OSPF Areas)
High bandwidth consumption from LSA flooding

theory:
Routing algorithms determine how packets are forwarded through a network by computing shortest paths between nodes.

DISTANCE VECTOR ROUTING (Bellman-Ford):
- Each router knows only the distance (cost/hop count) to each destination and the next hop.
- Routers exchange their FULL routing tables with directly connected neighbours periodically.
- Update rule (Bellman-Ford): d(u,v) = min over all w { c(u,w) + d(w,v) } where c(u,w) is the link cost to neighbour w.
- RIP (Routing Information Protocol) uses hop count as metric (max 15 hops; 16 = unreachable).
- Count-to-Infinity Problem: When a link fails, incorrect information slowly propagates, causing routers to count up to infinity. Mitigated by: Split Horizon, Poison Reverse, Hold-down timers.

LINK STATE ROUTING (Dijkstra's SPF):
- Each router knows the COMPLETE topology of the entire network.
- Step 1: Discover neighbours using Hello packets (Neighbor Discovery).
- Step 2: Create Link State Advertisements (LSA) — local link info.
- Step 3: Flood LSAs to ALL routers using reliable flooding with sequence numbers.
- Step 4: Each router builds an identical Link State Database (LSDB).
- Step 5: Run Dijkstra's SPF algorithm on the LSDB to find the shortest path tree.
- OSPF (Open Shortest Path First) uses bandwidth as metric and supports hierarchical areas.
keyPoints: Distance Vector (RIP): Bellman-Ford, shares FULL table with NEIGHBOURS only, slow convergence.
Link State (OSPF): Dijkstra's SPF, floods LOCAL link info to ALL routers, fast convergence.
DVR suffers from count-to-infinity problem — fixed by split horizon and route poisoning.
LSR requires more memory/CPU (stores LSDB + runs Dijkstra) but scales better with areas.
RIP max hop count = 15 (16 = infinity). OSPF has no hop limit, uses bandwidth as metric.
Bellman-Ford: d(u,v) = min over w { cost(u,w) + d(w,v) }.
useCases: Configuring RIP (small networks, simple setup) vs OSPF (enterprise, large ISP networks).
Understanding BGP (Border Gateway Protocol) — path vector routing between autonomous systems.
Exam questions on Dijkstra's algorithm execution tables and routing table updates.
